coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free sensor for Curcumin determination in real sample and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yang
Gong, Xiaojuan
Dong, Wenjuan
Zhou, Ruixin
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: A nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots (NP-Cdots) was fastly synthesized with glucose as the carbon source, 1,2-ethylenediamine as N-dopant and concentrated phosphoric acid as P-dopant. The as-synthesized NP-Cdots was utilized as a label-free sensor for determination of Curcumin (Cur). The proposed NP-Cdots-based fluorescence sensor was applied for sensitive detection of Cur in aqueous solution, achieving a linear range of 0.5–20 µmol/L and a detection limit of 58 nmol/L (21.37 ng/mL). The common amino acids and other drugs do not interfere with the detection of Cur, providing good selectivity. The constructed sensor was successfully applied to the determination of Cur in drinking water and the food samples with satisfactory results and the RSDs and recoveries were 0.08–5.39% and 95.2–105.2%, respectively. More importantly, the as-prepared NP-Cdots was used as effective fluorescent agent for cellular imaging without noticeable cytotoxicity. The proposed sensor is simple and practical, illustrating that the potential application of NP-Cdots for biosensing, food monitoring and cellular labeling and imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots
Label-free sensor
Curcumin
Food samples
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NP-Cdots)
A label-free sensor for Curcumin determination
MTT assays and multicolor cellular imaging
Determination of Curcumin in food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption (red line), fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission (magenta line) spectra of NP-Cdots in aqueous solution (Inset: photographs of NP-Cdots solution under visible light and UV light irradiation at 365 nm). The concentration of NP-Cdots is 0.15 mg/mL. (B) The emission spectra of NP-Cdots obtained under different excitation wavelengths.
(A) XPS full scan spectrum, (B) O1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum, (D) C1s spectrum, (E) P2p spectrum, and (F) FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared NP-Cdots.
(A) Typical TEM image and (B) the particle size distribution histogram of the as-synthesized NP-Cdots.
Comparison of fluorescence intensity of NP-Cdots (0.15 mg/mL) after the addition of (A) common amino acids (1.0 mmol/L) and (B) Cur and other drugs (1.0 mmol/L).

(A) Fluorescence quenching of NP-Cdots (0.15 mg/mL) in the presence of Cur (0.0–20 µmol/L). (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity of NP-Cdots (0.15 mg/mL) against Cur concentration in the range of 0.5–20 µmol/L. (C) The overlap between absorption spectrum of Cur and the excitation and emission spectra of NP-Cdots. (D) Fluorescence decay of NP-Cdots (0.15 mg/mL) in the absence and presence of Cur (20 µmol/L) as a function of time at excitation and emission wavelengths of 405/502 nm.

LSCM images of SiHa cells incubated with 0.5 mg/mL NP-Cdots at 37 °C for 2 h. (a) shows the bright field image of SiHa cells. (b), (c), (d) and (e) are cell images taken at excitation/emission wavelengths of 405/422 ± 25, 488/500 ± 25, 515/570 ± 25 and 543/650 ± 25 nm, respectively. (f), (g), (h), (i), (j), (k), (l), (m), (n) and (o) are the merged images of a and b, a and c, a and d, a and e, b and c, b and d, b and e, c and d, c and e and d and e, respectively. Scale bar: 40 µm.
Comparison of the proposed and typical methods employed for Cur detection.

row.entry: Ethyl acetate; sodium dodecyl sulfate; acetonitrile; tetrahydrofuran; formic acid
C18 column; λ = 270 nm; λ = 380 nm
TLC aluminium plates precoated with silica gel 60F−254; λ = 430 nm
Graphene-modified glassy carbon electrode; pH = 3.0; 0.1 mol/L H2SO4
Acetonitrile; poly (ᴅ,ʟ-lactide); soybean hydrogenated lecithin; castor oil; hydroxystearic acid-polyethylene glycol copolymer; poloxamer
BNCDs (prepared by boric acid, citric acid and monohydrate and urea)
NP-Cdots (prepared by glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine and concentrated phosphoric acid)
λex = 360 nm; λem = 450 nm; ultrapure water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Analytical results for Cur in real samples (n = 5, µmol/L); (B) Detection of Cur in real samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free sensor for Curcumin determination in real sample and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots (NP-Cdots) was fastly synthesized with glucose as the carbon source, 1,2-ethylenediamine as N-dopant and concentrated phosphoric acid as P-dopant. The as-synthesized NP-Cdots was utilized as a label-free sensor for determination of Curcumin (Cur). The proposed NP-Cdots-based fluorescence sensor was applied for sensitive detection of Cur in aqueous solution, achieving a linear range of 0.5–20 µmol/L and a detection limit of 58 nmol/L (21.37 ng/mL). The common amino acids and other drugs do not interfere with the detection of Cur, providing good selectivity. The constructed sensor was successfully applied to the determination of Cur in drinking water and the food samples with satisfactory results and the RSDs and recoveries were 0.08–5.39% and 95.2–105.2%, respectively. More importantly, the as-prepared NP-Cdots was used as effective fluorescent agent for cellular imaging without noticeable cytotoxicity. The proposed sensor is simple and practical, illustrating that the potential application of NP-Cdots for biosensing, food monitoring and cellular labeling and imaging.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin (Cur), 1,7-bis-(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)-1,6-heptadiene-2,5-dione, is a coloring agent [1,2] which derives from the rhizomes of turmeric. Cur is usually used as a common ingredient in spices, cosmetics and traditional Chinese medicine [3–5]. It has been well applied in the medical industry, which attributes to its biological and pharmacological activities including anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant, anti-carcinogenic and anti-HIV [6,7]. Because of the emphasis on the use of Cur in the food processing, medical industry and clinical therapy, numerous analytical assays have been developed for the quantification of Cur, including spectrophotometry [8], spectrofluorimetry [3], high-performance thin-layer chromatographic [9], high performance liquid chromatography [10], electrochemical technique [11], and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [12]. However, most of them require expensive equipment, time-consuming and complicated sample pretreatment. The spectrofluorimetry has attracted extensive attention because of its intrinsically advantages, such as high sensitivity, facile and rapid analysis as well as cost-effectiveness. As such, developing a simple and label-free spectrofluorimetry-based assay is necessary for routine Cur monitoring in food, medicine and pharmacology.

Among all the fluorescent materials, fluorescent nanomaterials have been widely studied in recent years. Compared with classic quantum dots and fluorescent molecules, carbon dots (Cdots) have been widespread applied as chem-probe, bio-sensor, and used for cellular imaging and labeling due to its dramatic merits such as simple synthetic route, favorable photoluminescence, good water solubility, excellent chemical stability and catalytic activity, no blinking fluorescence and eminent biocompatibility [13–15]. Easy to surface functionalization is one of the most notable feature of Cdots, which allows the doping of heteroatoms into Cdots to enhance its optical properties. In recent studies, doping with nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) have been extensively applied to improve the optical properties of Cdots [14,15]. Doping with N could introduce defect sites by replacing carbon atoms, providing potential active sites to enrich chemical activities of Cdots [14,16,17]. However, there are some defects of N-doped Cdots such as low-stable decentrality in water and weak resistance to metal ion interference, acid or base [18,19]. To avoid these drawbacks, P was frequently employed to dope the N-doped Cdots to form N and P dual-doped Cdots, which can distinctly improve the water decentrality. Recently, there were a few studies focusing on the NP-dual-doped Cdots, where aliphatic amine/NH3·H2O and phosphoric acid were employed as the N and P sources, respectively [20–22]. The doping ratios of N and/or P were relative low and the emissive wavelengths were in the blue region in these researches. Furthermore, these NP-dual-doped Cdots are sensitive to Cr(VI) [23] and Fe3+ [22,24] even under low concentration. However, the use of NP-dual-doped Cdots-based fluorescent sensor for the determination of Cur in real samples has been rarely reported. Therefore, it is an urgent need to construct a simple, rapid, sensitive and label-free fluorescent sensor for Cur detection.

In this study, we reported a green and straight forward procedure for the fabrication of nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots (NP-Cdots) by simple mixing glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine and concentrated phosphoric acid, which served as carbon source, N doping agent and P doping agent, respectively. The NP-Cdots serve as IFE-based fluorescent sensor for Cur detection based on the remarkable fluorescence quenching mechanism. The effective fluorescent sensor for sensitive and selective determination of Cur achieved a detection limit as low as 58 nmol/L, which is much lower than other reported values [3,7]. More importantly, the as-constructed label-free fluorescent sensor was successfully used for the detection of Cur in real sample. Finally, NP-Cdots with cyan fluorescence was used as an effective fluorescent agent for multicolor bioimaging of SiHa cells, indicating that it is a promising candidate for bioanalysis and bioimaging.

Glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), concentration phosphoric acid (H3PO4), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and other chemical reagents were obtained from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), fifteen kinds of common amino acids (Try, Tyr, Val, Phe, Leu, Gly, Asp, Met, Ala, L-Cys, DL-Cys, Glu, Pro, Ser and Thr) and fifteen kinds of drugs (folic acid (FA), morin hydrate (MH), ascorbic acid (AA), Ibuprofen (Ibu), Glutathione (GSH), Tetracycline (Tet), Hydrochloride (DA), Guanosine hydrate (GH), Adenosine (Ade), Human serum albumin (HSA), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Gardenoside (Gar), Ofloxacin (OFL), Doxorubicin (DOX) and Curcumin (Cur)) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). All reagents are analytical grade or above, which were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water (≥ 18.25 MΩ cm) from a MilliQ Plus System (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) were used to prepared all aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NP-Cdots)
ce:section.ce:para: The EDA (6.0 mL) was mixed with 0.4 g glucose in a 100 mL beaker, and then the concentrated H3PO4 (2.0 mL) was added to the mixture dropwise without stirring. The reaction mixture was quickly foamed by the acid-base neutralization spontaneous heat. It was then gradually cooled down to room temperature within 6 min. The crude product was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 72 h through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off, MWCO: 1000 Da). Finally, NP-Cdots in dark brown color were collected and lyophilized to obtain the dry NP-Cdots product.

The apparatus and parameters of all characterizations (transmission electron microscopic (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), elemental analysis, UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence spectra, quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime) were referred our previously published papers [20,23,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: A label-free sensor for Curcumin determination
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of NP-Cdots in ultrapure water was 0.3 mg/mL. Then, the aqueous solutions containing fifteen kinds of amino acids (Try, Tyr, Val, Phe, Leu, Gly, Asp, Met, Ala, L-Cys, DL-Cys, Glu, Pro, Ser and Thr) and fifteen kinds of drugs (FA, MH, AA, Ibu, GSH, Tet, DA, GH, Ade, HSA, BSA, Gar, OFL, DOX and Cur) at a concentration of 0.01 mol/L were prepared, respectively. To evaluate the selectivity of NP-Cdots, each 300 µL of the above amino acid or drug was mingled with 2.7 mL NP-Cdots, and the PL intensities were measured. To access the linear range and the detection limit of Cur, 0.3 mL of the solutions containing different concentrations of Cur was mixed with 2.7 mL NP-Cdots. The sample obtained by mixing 0.3 mL of ultrapure water with 2.7 mL NP-Cdots was used as a control sample. All the samples were excited at 403 nm, and the fluorescence emission were recorded at 502 nm.
All real samples were purchased from a local supermarket in Taiyuan, China, and used immediately after treatment.

Ginger juice and Capsicum chinense juice were obtained by plastic juicer. Curry powder, Ground ginger and Mustard were dissolved in ultrapure water to form 0.1 g/mL solution, respectively. All of the above solutions were centrifuged in a refrigerated centrifuge at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 mm pore size filter membrane and then diluted to an appropriate concentration in the detection range with ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assays and multicolor cellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Human cervical carcinoma SiHa cell was chosen to test the cell cytotoxicity and cellular imaging of NP-Cdots. The incubation concentration of NP-Cdots is 0.5 mg/mL for cellular imaging. The experimental procedures were referred to our previously published papers [20,23,25].

Water-soluble fluorescent NP-Cdots were simply fabricated by an acid-base reaction heat strategy using glucose, EDA and H3PO4 as carbon source, nitrogen dopant and phosphorus dopant, respectively. The as-synthesized NP-Cdots solution was yellow transparent in visible light and showed a bright cyan fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (inset in Fig. 1A).

The optical properties of the as-prepared NP-Cdots were explored and the optical spectra are revealed in Fig. 1. As shown in the blank dotted line of Fig. 1A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NP-Cdots possesses two absorption bands with 275 nm and 336 nm, which are likely attributed to the n→π
* transition of the C˭O band and the excited defect surface states induced by N and P heteroatoms, respectively [23,26]. The fluorescence spectra of NP-Cdots are depicted in Fig. 1A (blue and magenta lines), which indicates that the NP-Cdots exhibits the excitation and emission wavelengths at 403 nm and 502 nm, respectively. To further investigate the optical properties of the as-fabricated NP-Cdots, the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior was probed and is illustrated in Fig. 1B. The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of NP-Cdots is attributed to the surface state which affects the band gap of NP-Cdots [20,23,26]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S1, the QY of the as-fabricated NP-Cdots is calculated to be 9.59%. The photostability of the fabricated NP-Cdots was investigated by extended detecting of fluorescence intensity at 502 nm. The results (Fig. S2) indicate the inappreciable variation of fluorescence intensity of NP-Cdots under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiation about 5 h.

To examine the surface states and chemical composition of the NP-Cdots, the elemental analysis and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were conducted. As shown in Table S1A, the as-prepared NP-Cdots is comprised of C 18.79, H 7.42, N 17.01, P 16.54 and O (calculated) 40.24 wt%, indicating that they are co-doped with nitrogen and phosphorus. Table S1B describes the relative number of atoms which is expressed according to the elemental contents. The empirical formula for a NP-Cdots is approximately C3H14N2PO5. The XPS results further demonstrates the doping of N and P into NP-Cdots successfully. The XPS survey scan of NP-Cdots ( Fig. 2A) exhibits five peaks at 528.6, 397.7, 283.4, 191.5 and 131.3 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, P2s and P2p, respectively. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B) was disassembled into four peaks at 530.0, 530.6, 531.2 and 531.9 eV, which are associated with *O˭C-O, O˭P, C-OH/C-O-C and O-P, respectively. The N1s spectrum of NP-Cdots in Fig. 2C depicts four peaks at 397.9, 398.4, 399.0 and 399.8 eV, corresponding to C-N-C, N-(C)3, pyridinic-like N and pyrrolic-like N, respectively. The high resolution C1s spectrum of the NP-Cdots (Fig. 2D) exhibits five peaks at 284.3, 284.9, 285.5, 286.1 and 287.3 eV, which are attributed to the C˭C, C-C/C-P, C-OH/C-O-C, C˭N and C˭O groups, respectively. The P2p spectrum (Fig. 2E) presented P2p3/2 (133.3 eV) and P-C binding motifs (133.8 eV). Therefore, all data indicate that N and P have been successfully doped into the graphite structure of the NP-Cdots.

To further identify the doping form of N and P in the NP-Cdots and characterize the functional groups of the as-prepared NP-Cdots, the FTIR spectrum of NP-Cdots were acquired and is depicted in Fig. 2F. It is observed that the sharp peak at 960 cm−1 is attributed to P-O-H stretching bond, the sharp peak at 1075 cm−1 is ascribed to the P-O-R (R=C, N, P) stretching, and the peak at 1148 cm−1 is ascribed to P=O stretching bond [20,27,28]. In addition, the other characteristic peaks at 1408, 1567 and 1639 cm−1 corresponding to -CH3 umbrella twisting, C˭N stretching and C˭O stretching, respectively are identified [27,28]. More importantly, three broad absorption peaks at 2500–2760, 2858 and 3473 cm−1 are attributed to P-OH bending, -C-H stretching and C-OH stretching, respectively, revealing that the NP-Cdots have plenty of hydroxyl groups [27]. The zeta potential of NP-Cdots (Fig. S3) is − 9.3 mV. In essence, the abundant functional groups not only endow NP-Cdots with negative charges, but also improve the hydrophilicity and stability of NP-Cdots in aqueous system.

The structure and morphology of NP-Cdots were further measured and characterized by TEM. The representative TEM image is shown in Fig. 3A, which illustrated that the NP-Cdots are of primary orbicular morphology and is spread evenly on the copper grid surface. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 3B) obtained by counting about 150 particles indicates that the size of NP-Cdots is between 2.1 and 5.25 nm with an average particle size of 3.5 ± 0.2 nm, which demonstrates the explanation of the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of NP-Cdots. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) results (Fig. S4) show that the average hydrodynamic particle size of NP-Cdots is 10.15 ± 0.8 nm, indicating that the hydrodynamic diameter is larger than that determined from the TEM, which further states that the as-prepared NP-Cdots are highly dispersible without any aggregation in aqueous solution.

As the optical properties of NP-Cdots arise from particle surface states, different pH values may have distinct effects on the fluorescence intensity of NP-Cdots. To find an optimal pH value for the following experiment, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of NP-Cdots was studied. The fluorescence intensities of NP-Cdots remain almost constant when increasing pH from 3 to 10 (Fig. S5A), especially in the physiological pH range of 7.0–7.5. Considering the normal physiological pH values, ultrapure water (pH = 7.0) was chosen for all subsequent detection assays. The NP-Cdots is also very stable in high-ionic strength (in terms of the concentration of KCl) solutions (Fig. S5B), which is beneficial for the use of NP-Cdots in salt solutions. In addition, the as-prepared NP-Cdots reveals excellent photo-stability, as the fluorescence intensity does not change even after continuous excitation with a UV lamp for 300 min (Fig. S2). All these features make NP-Cdots a good candidate for fluorescence nanosensor, bioimaging or other biomedical applications.

For bioimaging, it is required that the selected fluorescent marker should possess not only optical merits but also low cytotoxicity [13,18–20,25–27]. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of the as-synthesized NP-Cdots, the MTT assays were carried out to measure the viability of SiHa cells treated with NP-Cdots. As shown in Fig. S6, the cell viability is defined as 100% in absence of NP-Cdots. When different concentrations (50, 100, 200, 400 and 800 µg mL−1) of NP-Cdots were added and incubated with the SiHa cells for 48 h, it was observed that decline of the cell viability is less than 10%, which clearly demonstrates the intrinsically low cytotoxicity of the NP-Cdots.

The as-prepared NP-Cdots was used as a highly sensitive and label-free Cur sensor. The fluorescence quenching effect of various amino acids and drugs on NP-Cdots was investigated. Different amino acids including Try, Tyr, Val, Phe, Leu, Gly, Asp, Met, Ala, L-Cys, DL-Cys, Glu, Pro, Ser and Thr and drugs including FA, MH, AA, Ibu, GSH, Tet, DA, GH, Ade, HSA, BSA, Gar, OFL, DOX and Cur of each at a concentration of 1.0 mmol/L were reacted with a NP-Cdots solution (0.3 mg/mL). Fig. 4A and B display the relative change in fluorescence intensity of NP-Cdots in the presence of various amino acids and drugs, respectively. It is seen that all the amino acids and drugs cause the slight changes (86–111%) and (86–109%) in fluorescence intensity of NP-Cdots, which clearly demonstrates that the as-fabricated NP-Cdots sensor presents an excellent selectivity for Cur sensing over the other amino acids and drugs.

Fig. 5A depicts the changes in fluorescence of NP-Cdots in the presence of different concentrations of Cur. The inset of Fig. 5A displays the photographs of NP-Cdots solution taken under a UV lamp excitation at 365 nm. It can be seen that the fluorescence color of NP-Cdots solution changes from green to colorless as the Cur concentration increases from 0.0 to 20 µmol/L. The good linear relationship of ∆F (Eq. (1)) and Cur concentration is observed from 0.5 to 20 µmol/L (Fig. 5B) with the linear equation of (2),
(1)
Δ F =
F
0
− F
(2)
Δ F = 45.39
c
( Cur )
+ 2.25
where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of NP-Cdots at excitation/emission wavelengths of 403/502 nm in absence and presence of Cur, respectively. The LOD of the proposed sensor was calculated to be 58 nmol/L (21.37 ng/mL) based on three times the standard deviation rule (3),
(3)
LOD
= 3
σ
/ S
where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements, and S is the slope of the calibration graph. These results imply that the proposed "turn-off" fluorescent sensor based on NP-Cdots is suitable for the label-free determination of Cur in real sample. Furthermore, the analytical features comparison of typical reported methods [7,9–12,29–33] employed for Cur detection is listed in Table 1.

Inner filter effect (IFE) phenomenon usually happens between the absorber and fluorophore, arising from the absorption of the excitation and emission light by absorbers in the detection system. The highly effective IFE desires the complementary overlap of the absorber's absorption band with the excitation and emission bands of the fluorophore. Hence, it is important to choose a suitable absorber and fluorophore in the IFE-based fluorescent assays [34]. The Cur shows a wide absorption peak at around 315–527 nm (red line in Fig. 5C). The as-prepared NP-Cdots was chosen as the fluorophore because both the excitation (403 nm) and emission (502 nm) spectra of NP-Cdots reveals precise overlap with the absorption band of Cur, illustrating Cur shields excitation and emission light from NP-Cdots. Consequently, the fluorescence of NP-Cdots could be quenched by the absorbance enhancement of Cur, which undertakes that the IFE occurs in a highly efficient way. More importantly, in order to further understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism between Cur absorber and NP-Cdots fluorophore, the lifetimes of NP-Cdots in the absence and presence of Cur were measured and the decay spectra fitted with bi-exponential curve are displayed in Fig. 5D. The relevant data are summarized in Table S2. In the absence of Cur (red line in Fig. 5D), the average lifetime value of NP-Cdots is 7.01 ns (χ2 = 1.272) with lifetimes of τ1 = 3.37 ns (44.78%) and τ2 = 9.96 ns (55.22%). In the presence of 20 µmol/L Cur (blue line in Fig. 5D), the average fluorescence lifetime of the NP-Cdots is 7.03 ns (χ2 = 1.193) with lifetimes of τ1 = 3.49 ns (46.99%) and τ2 = 10.16 ns (53.01%). The almost no changes of lifetime indicates that there is no significant electron-transfer process between NP-Cdots and Cur, illustrating that the fluorescence quenching of NP-Cdots by Cur is attributed to the IFE mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Curcumin in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The NP-Cdots-based fluorescent sensing platform was applied for Cur determination in various kinds of food samples. The recoveries of this method were concluded by spiking a series of different known concentrations of Cur. As depicted in Table 2, the Cur contents in Drinking water, Ground ginger, Curry powder, Ginger juice, Mustard and Capsicum chinense juice detected by the fabricated "off-on" sensor are 0.0, 4.63, 3.04, 4.83, 2.27 and 0.78 µmol/L, respectively, with the RSDs are between 0.65% and 5.29%. It is worth to note that the quantitative spike recoveries for the detection of Cur in these above food samples ranges from 95.2% to 105.2% with the RSDs < 5.39% (n = 5). Thus, the NP-Cdots is promising in label-free monitoring of the amount of Cur in real samples.

The as-synthesized NP-Cdots was then used as fresh fluorescence label for cellular labeling and imaging applications. The SiHa cells, which are usually used as model cells, were used to survey the intracellular imaging. The cellular uptakes of NP-Cdots were viewed via laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM). As displayed in Fig. 6, the NP-Cdots is internalized and well-decentralized into the cytoplasm region, and a small part of NP-Cdots enters into the cell nucleus. In addition, the cells stained with NP-Cdots emit most intensely blue, green, yellow and red emissions. The LSCM images of SiHa cells cultured with NP-Cdots possess normal morphology (a of Fig. 6), implying that NP-Cdots are biocompatible and have minimum cytotoxicity to cells. These cells emit blue (b of Fig. 6), green (c of Fig. 6), yellow (d of Fig. 6) and red (e of Fig. 6) emissions when they are stimulated by 405, 488, 515 and 543 nm lasers, respectively. The merged images of a and b (f of Fig. 6), a and c (g of Fig. 6), a and d (h of Fig. 6), a and e (i of Fig. 6), b and c (j of Fig. 6), b and d (k of Fig. 6), b and e (l of Fig. 6), c and d (m of Fig. 6), c and e (n of Fig. 6) and d and e (o of Fig. 6) illustrate the excellent ability of NP-Cdots in permeating the cell membrane. These observations demonstrate that the NP-Cdots could be utilized as an eminent in vitro cellular imaging agent.

Herein, we developed a simple, fast and one-step way to fabricate fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots (NP-Cdots) by a simply mixing glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine and concentrated phosphoric acid. The as-synthesized NP-Cdots display a remarkably rapid, sensitive and selective response towards Cur based on the inner filter effect. The fluorescence sensor based on NP-Cdots possess numerous advantages in mild determination condition (in ultrapure water), rapider detection (in 60 s), wider linear range (0.5–20 µmol/L) and higher sensitivity (LOD = 58 nmol/L). Furthermore, the as-constructed sensor was successfully applied for the determination of Cur in drinking water and the food samples with satisfactory results achieved, illustrating its possibility in label-free and real-time monitoring of Cur. Ultimately, NP-Cdots could be utilized as a fluorescent reagent for in vitro imaging. Combined its simplicity and fast speed, it is anticipated that NP-Cdots could be potentially applied in a wide range of applications, such as food quality monitoring, medical quality supervision, and biomedical controlling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21705101 and 21575084) and the Postgraduate Student Education Innovation Project (No. 2016BY005), all of which are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.02.060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probe for detection of curcumin based on the inner filter effect
Curcumin: an anti-inflammatory molecule from a curry spice on the path to cancer treatment
Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for determination of curcumin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Validation of an ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) technique for the quantitative determination of curcumin in poly(ʟ-lactic acid) nanoparticles
Stability-indicating HPTIC determination of curcumin in bulk drug and pharmaceutical formulations
A simple RP-HPLC method for the simultaneous determination of curcumin and its prodrug, curcumin didecanoate, in rat plasma and the application to pharmacokinetic study
The electrochemical characterization of curcumin and its selective detection in Curcuma using a graphene-modified electrode
Qualitative and quantitative analysis of curcuminoids in herbal medicines derived from Curcuma species
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Fluorescent carbon dots with tunable negative charges for bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment
Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications
The doping of reduced graphene oxide with nitrogen and its effect on the quenching of the material's photoluminescence
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
